By 2014, though, the average MLB game took over three hours to complete.
Other leagues have experienced similar issues.
This was up 10 minutes from 2015 and 21 minutes from 2014.
While coaching staffs can signal pitcher or batter to pursue certain tactics, the execution of the play itself is a series of solitary acts.

Cricket is more similar to baseball than many other team sports in this regard: while the individual focus in cricket is mitigated by the importance of the batting partnership and the practicalities of tandem running, it is enhanced by the fact that a batsman may occupy the wicket for an hour or much more. There is no statistical equivalent in cricket for the fielding error and thus less emphasis on personal responsibility in this area of play.
Major league teams often skirt even this rule.
There are no rules at all that address the height of fences or other structures at the edge of the outfield.
Similarly, there are no regulations at all concerning the dimensions of foul territory.
These variations can make the difference between a double and a triple or inside-the-park home run. The surface of the field is also unregulated.
While football and soccer players deal with similar variations of field surface and stadium covering, the size and shape of their fields are much more standardized.

These physical variations create a distinctive set of playing conditions at each ballpark. Other local factors, such as altitude and climate, can also significantly affect play. A given stadium may acquire a reputation as a pitcher's park or a hitter's park, if one or the other discipline notably benefits from its unique mix of elements. The most exceptional park in this regard is Coors Field, home of the Colorado Rockies. Its high altitude—5,282 feet (1,610 m) above sea level—is partly responsible for giving it the strongest hitter's park effect in the major leagues due to the low air pressure.
Each play is discrete and has a relatively small number of possible outcomes.
The rules provide detailed criteria to promote consistency.
The term is also used to refer directly to new statistics themselves.
Wins Above Replacement (WAR) measures number of additional wins his team has achieved above the number of expected team wins if that player were substituted with a replacement-level player.
A century later, the situation was very different.

In the late 1900s and early 2000s, baseball's position compared to football in the United States moved in contradictory directions. In 2008, MLB set a revenue record of $6.5 billion, matching the NFL's revenue for the first time in decades. A new MLB revenue record of more than $10 billion was set in 2017. On the other hand, the percentage of American sports fans polled who named baseball as their favorite sport was 9%, compared to pro football at 37%.

Because there are so many more major league games played, there is no comparison in overall attendance. In 2008, total attendance at major league games was the second-highest in history: 78.6 million, 0.7% off the record set the previous year. The following year, amid the U.S. recession, attendance fell by 6.6% to 73.4 million. Eight years later, it dropped under 73 million. Attendance at games held under the Minor League Baseball umbrella set a record in 2008, with 43.3 million. While MLB games have not drawn the same national TV viewership as football games, MLB games are dominant in teams' local markets and regularly lead all programs in primetime in their markets during the summer.
In 2017, 83 of the 868 players on MLB Opening Day rosters (and disabled lists) were from the country.

While baseball has long been the island's primary athletic pastime, its once well-attended professional winter league has declined in popularity since 1990, when young Puerto Rican players began to be included in the major leagues' annual first-year player draft.

Total NPB attendance for the year was approximately 20 million. While in the preceding two decades, MLB attendance grew by 50 percent and revenue nearly tripled, the comparable NPB figures were stagnant. There are concerns that MLB's growing interest in acquiring star Japanese players will hurt the game in their home country. Revenue figures are not released for the country's amateur system. Similarly, according to one official pronouncement, the sport's governing authority "has never taken into account attendance ... because its greatest interest has always been the development of athletes". In Taiwan, baseball is one of the most widely spectated sports, with the origins dating back to Japanese rule.
The number of players has fallen since the 1990s, when 3 million children took part in Little League Baseball annually.

A varsity baseball team is an established part of physical education departments at most high schools and colleges in the United States. In 2015, nearly half a million high schoolers and over 34,000 collegians played on their schools' baseball teams. By early in the 20th century, intercollegiate baseball was Japan's leading sport.

The final rounds of the two annual tournaments—the National High School Baseball Invitational Tournament in the spring, and the even more important National High School Baseball Championship in the summer—are broadcast around the country. The tournaments are known, respectively, as Spring Koshien and Summer Koshien after the 55,000-capacity stadium where they are played. In Cuba, baseball is a mandatory part of the state system of physical education, which begins at age six. Talented children as young as seven are sent to special district schools for more intensive training—the first step on a ladder whose acme is the national baseball team.

Dozens of English-language idioms have been derived from baseball; in particular, the game is the source of a number of widely used sexual euphemisms. The first networked radio broadcasts in North America were of the 1922 World Series: famed sportswriter Grantland Rice announced play-by-play from New York City's Polo Grounds on WJZ–Newark, New Jersey, which was connected by wire to WGY–Schenectady, New York, and WBZ–Springfield, Massachusetts. The baseball cap has become a ubiquitous fashion item not only in the United States and Japan, but also in countries where the sport itself is not particularly popular, such as the United Kingdom. Baseball has inspired many works of art and entertainment. One of the first major examples, Ernest Thayer's poem "Casey at the Bat", appeared in 1888. A wry description of the failure of a star player in what would now be called a "clutch situation", the poem became the source of vaudeville and other staged performances, audio recordings, film adaptations, and an opera, as well as a host of sequels and parodies in various media. There have been many baseball movies, including the Academy Award–winning The Pride of the Yankees (1942) and the Oscar nominees The Natural (1984) and Field of Dreams (1989). The American Film Institute's selection of the ten best sports movies includes The Pride of the Yankees at number 3 and Bull Durham (1988) at number 5.

The baseball-inspired comedic sketch "Who's on First? ", popularized by Abbott and Costello in 1938, quickly became famous. Six decades later, Time named it the best comedy routine of the 20th century. Literary works connected to the game include the short fiction of Ring Lardner and novels such as Bernard Malamud's The Natural (the source for the movie), Robert Coover's The Universal Baseball Association, Inc., J. Henry Waugh, Prop., John Grisham's Calico Joe and W. P. Kinsella's Shoeless Joe (the source for Field of Dreams).
Among the celebrated nonfiction books in the field are Lawrence S. Ritter's The Glory of Their Times, Roger Kahn's The Boys of Summer, and Michael Lewis's Moneyball.
Baseball has also inspired the creation of new cultural forms.

A typical example featured an image of a baseball player on one side and advertising for a business on the other. In the early 1900s they were produced widely as promotional items by tobacco and confectionery companies. The 1930s saw the popularization of the modern style of baseball card, with a player photograph accompanied on the rear by statistics and biographical data.
Rotisserie-style play quickly became a phenomenon.

The field boomed with increasing Internet access and new fantasy sports-related websites. By 2008, 29.9 million people in the United States and Canada were playing fantasy sports, spending $800 million on the hobby.

In Wales and Merseyside, a strong community game had already developed with skills and plays more in keeping with the American game and the Welsh began to informally adopt the name "baseball" (Pêl Fas), to reflect the American style. By the 1890s, calls were made to follow the success of other working class sports (like Rugby in Wales and Soccer in Merseyside) and adopt a distinct set of rules and bureaucracy.

The game's history is rich with transformation, from its disputed origins and amateur beginnings to its professionalization, integration, internationalization, and deep-rooted presence in popular culture. It is not only a sport but a reflection of broader social, economic, and political currents that have shaped the modern world.
While a common legend attributes the invention of baseball to Abner Doubleday in Cooperstown, New York, in 1839, this account has been widely debunked by historians.

Games such as rounders from England and various forms of stickball played in colonial America laid the foundation for what would become baseball. In the early 19th century, informal versions of the game were played in northeastern cities such as New York, Boston, and Philadelphia, often with different local rules.
As the game gained popularity in the mid-19th century, it began transitioning from a recreational pastime to a competitive sport.
Following the war, baseball clubs formed throughout the United States, and the first professional team, the Cincinnati Red Stockings, was established in 1869.
It introduced a more centralized and disciplined structure, promoting stability and attracting serious investors.

The integrity of the game, however, was not immune to scandal. The most notorious event was the 1919 Black Sox Scandal, in which eight members of the Chicago White Sox were accused of conspiring to fix the World Series in exchange for money from gamblers. Though acquitted in court, they were permanently banned from baseball by Commissioner Kenesaw Mountain Landis, who was appointed to restore public confidence in the sport. Landis wielded nearly absolute power and enforced a strict code of conduct, laying the foundation for the commissioner’s role in overseeing the integrity of the game.

While African American players and teams existed in the 19th century, they were gradually excluded from professional leagues, leading to the establishment of the Negro Leagues in the early 20th century.

Players such as Satchel Paige, Josh Gibson, and Cool Papa Bell achieved legendary status despite being denied the opportunity to compete in the major leagues. The color barrier was finally broken in 1947 when Jackie Robinson debuted with the Brooklyn Dodgers, becoming the first African American to play in MLB in the modern era.

The 1950s and 1960s saw teams relocating to new markets as the United States experienced population shifts and urban growth. The Dodgers and Giants moved from New York to Los Angeles and San Francisco respectively, bringing major league baseball to the West Coast. New franchises were established, and the game increasingly embraced innovations such as night games, television broadcasting, and international scouting.
Baseball also became more international during the 20th century.

This analytical approach revolutionized team management and influenced how fans and commentators understand the sport. Innovations in equipment, training, and medical treatment further enhanced performance and longevity for players.

The steroid era of the late 1990s and early 2000s cast a shadow over the game, as numerous high-profile players were implicated in the use of performance-enhancing drugs. Congressional hearings, suspensions, and stricter testing policies were introduced to address the issue and restore credibility.

The Little League World Series, begun in 1947, remains a major event showcasing young talent from around the world. College baseball is also significant, with the NCAA tournament and the College World Series drawing considerable attention.
Iconic ballparks like Fenway Park, Wrigley Field, and Yankee Stadium have become landmarks, and baseball has inspired countless works of literature, film, and art.
Advanced metrics, instant replay, pitch-tracking systems, and global media coverage have transformed the experience for fans and professionals alike.

Once the ore body has been identified a shaft is sunk in the vicinity of the ore veins, and crosscuts are driven horizontally to the veins at various levels, usually every 100 to 150 metres. Similar tunnels, known as drifts, are driven along the ore veins from the crosscut. To extract the ore, the next step is to drive tunnels, known as raises when driven upwards and winzes when driven downward, through the deposit from level to level. Raises are subsequently used to develop the stopes where the ore is mined from the veins.
The stope, which is the workshop of the mine, is the excavation from which the ore is extracted.

In the "cut and fill" or "open stoping" method, the space remaining following removal of ore after blasting is filled with waste rock and cement. In the "shrinkage" method, only sufficient broken ore is removed via the chutes below to allow miners working from the top of the pile to drill and blast the next layer to be broken off, eventually leaving a large hole. The method known as "room and pillar" is used for thinner, flatter ore bodies. In this method the ore body is first divided into blocks by intersecting drives, removing ore while so doing, and then systematically removing the blocks, leaving enough ore for roof support.
Heap leaching is generally economically feasible only for oxide ore deposits. Oxidation of sulfide deposits occurs during the geological process called weathering.

If the economic and geological conditions are right, the mining company will level large areas of land with a small gradient, layering it with thick plastic (usually HDPE or LLDPE), sometimes with clay, silt or sand beneath the plastic liner. The extracted ore will typically be run through a crusher and placed in heaps atop the plastic. The leaching agent will then be sprayed on the ore for 30–90 days.
As different heaps will yield different concentrations, the solution is pumped to a mixing plant that is carefully monitored.

Pricing can be as simple as a single fixed price, or based on various reference prices with economic corrections built in.

In base-escalated contracts, the buyer and seller agree on a base price that escalates over time on the basis of an agreed-upon formula, which may take economic indices, such as GDP or inflation factors, into consideration.
A spot market contract usually consists of just one delivery and is typically priced at or near the published spot market price at the time of purchase.

Long-term contract terms range from 2–10 years, but they typically run for 3–5 years, with the first delivery due within 24 months of contract award. They may also include a clause that allows the buyer to vary the size of each delivery within prescribed limits. For example, delivery quantities may vary from the prescribed annual volume by ±15%.
Typically, a fuel buyer from power utilities will contract separately with suppliers at each step of the process.

The utilities believe—rightly or wrongly—that these options offer them the best price and service. They will typically retain two or three suppliers for each stage of the fuel cycle, who compete for their business by tender. Sellers consist of suppliers in each of the four stages as well as brokers and traders.

Harry yelled, grabbing at her robes. Hermione’s feet hit the hard ground, running in tandem to her heartbeat as the prophecies shattered around them.

She could hear more yelling, screaming against the rising cacophony as shelves began shuddering from the impact of their spells, knocking into one-another. Looking over shoulder, she glanced as another Death Eater appeared, reaching out to grab at Harry’s shoulder as the wand pulled back, a curse almost spoken––'Stupefy!'

They ran on, Neville wheezing for breath beside her. 'Come on,' she said, grabbing him. 'Come on, we have to––' Neville threw a stupefy behind her and with a thud, she heard the spell hit its target. Without looking back she smiled and tugged him further, up to where Harry was entering the doorway. Neville entered first and then her, and Harry turned, pressing the door shut behind them.
Satisfied when the door sealed itself with a squelching noise.

Harry asked, panting for breath. Hermione’s eyes scanned the room, her stomach dropping as she realised, 'They must have gone the wrong way!' Harry paled and then went back to the door, pressing hard against to it to listen. She watched his face grow grave as voice mumbled through the door in what sounded like a shouting of orders.
He seemed to be the ring leader of all this, though Hermione wasn’t sure why. None of this was what they had expected and Hermione hated herself for it.

Harry wasn’t his master, he had free reign to lie and misguide as he liked to them. Whether by coincidence or otherwise, You-Know-Who’s plan had come to fruition and she should have known. Harry was going to die if they didn’t get help.

She asked out loud, feeling herself tremble. 'Well, we don’t stand here waiting for them to find us, for a start,' Harry said sharply. 'Let’s get away from this door.' Swallowing, Hermione nodded as she shot a glance to Neville. He stood firm, his expression brave as he gave a slight nod his head.
They were almost at the exit in the circular hallway when a thud! echoed behind them from the door she’d charmed shut.
Did they teach nothing at your school?'

The walls seemed to shudder before growing quiet. Holding a hand over her mouth, she forced herself to breath slowly, hoping they couldn’t hear the sound of her heart as the telltale noise of heels of stone floors echoed the room.
Bellatrix laughed, 'No, no, I don’t think so.
Hermione watched as the boots stepped near her, their robes swishing across the ground.

Hermione crawled forward, moving to aim her wand at the other, only to see Bellatrix’s wand pointing back at her. She was so close that Hermione could see the small blond hairs on the back of her hand.

Both Bellatrix’s and Harry’s wands flew out of their hands, they scrambled,  wrestling one another before untangling and running to grab their wands. Hermione could hear the spells being cast but hadn’t moved from where she was, under the desk. Her breath was tight in her chest as she still pictured Bellatrix’s eyes, watching her lips curl around the word.
Pushing up to her feet, Hermione ran after them and pointed her wand, screaming 'STUPEFY!'
The jet of red light hit Bellatrix in chest and froze her.
Shaking herself from the bizarre scene, she pointed her wand to where Harry’s had fallen and summoned it, frantic as she snatched it from the air and threw it back at Harry.

'Right, let’s get out of—'
'Look out!'

Hermione turned, pointing her wand ready to cast, only to realise he was staring at the head of the Bellatrix in the bell jar. It was shrinking, the skin softening, her teeth returning to a white, longer appearance as she rapidly gained youth—slowly it began to slide down her body as well. 'It’s Time,' Hermione realised.
She didn’t seem to look for her wand, and for that Hermione was thankful.
'We should kill her,' Neville said, a sudden boldness in his step––but a crash distracted them from nearby. Harry turned to its source, 'Ron?'

Bellatrix sat, stunned as Harry tossed her a look and grabbed Neville’s arm. 'Come on, we have to find the others.' And then he turned to look at her. Hermione nodded, going to leave when something stopped her. Bellatrix had snatched at her wrist, holding her still.

She asked, her voice no longer the cruel, sharpness that it had been. Harry looked at her, urging with his eyes to leave her. Another crash fell and the noise from the prophecy room grew louder. Hermione looked to the source, her heart beating fast in her chest. Someone had heard their fight and was coming to investigate. 'I have to go,' Hermione said, tugging her hand away from the witch’s. Following Harry, she ran out of the room, but it didn’t deter Bellatrix. As Hermione cast a look behind her, the woman––now a similar age to them––was following, her eyes wide, brow pinched with focus.
Veering left, Harry burst into a small, dark office and slammed the door behind them.

The two dark wizards gave a small, triumph cry and yelled: 'Impedimenta!' Hermione flew backwards, watching as Harry and Neville flew in an opposing direction from her, before she crashed into a bookshelf.
She cast, hitting the man in the throat. The force of the spell had him stumbling back as another Death Eater stepped forward.

At once, his arms and legs snapped together and fell forwards, face down on the rug. Hermione laughed, moving to stand as she turned to Harry. 'Well done—' Hermione went to say, only to feel something knock her to the side. Gasping, she looked as Bellatrix, had flung a curse back at the Death Eater, deflecting what had almost hit Hermione.

There was a quiet that followed as Hermione looked from the Death Eater to Bellatrix, surprised as the woman relaxed, holding her wand loosely at her side as if she hadn’t just been fighting them all not five minutes ago. 'You...you saved me,' Hermione said. Bellatrix turned, looking at her with a frown. 'He was going to kill you,' she said, rather sharply. 'Or try to––it was a pathetic cast.'
Bellatrix’s mouth opened and then she frowned.
Neville was standing beside him with a bloody nose, discarding a broken wand as he tried to avoid looking at them.
'We can’t take her with us, Hermione.' 'Harry, I don’t think she remembers,' she said.
The woman’s mouth parted, as if she was about to respond, when she paused. Hermione watched a bemused expression cross the witch’s face before she frowned.
'But you seem to know who I am.'

'We’re almost at the exit,' Harry said. 'If she really doesn’t know...if she’s forgotten...' he sighed. 'I don’t know if I believe her, but you can take the lift. Sound the alarm and get out of here.'
'I don’t want her coming with me.
I brought you all in this mess and it’s my responsibility to get out out.' 'Harry, you can’t,' she argued. 'I’ll go with him,' Neville assured. 'But...you should come with us, Hermione.

She wanted to go with them, but as she looked to Bellatrix, she wondered if taking her, as she was, out of the fight was better. She didn’t know how long it would last, but if it was Time, as she suspected, if it had changed her brain along with everything else, it would make sense that that the part of the brain that held memories would have transfigured along with everything else, likely 'purging’ all of her memories in the process...or at least the brain pathway to them. 'I’ll get help,' Hermione assured.
'Don’t be afraid to curse anyone that crosses you.' Hermione swallowed, nodding as she looked to the witch. 'You won’t curse me, will you?'
'If I was going to curse you, I would still say I wasn’t.'
Hermione didn’t know what else to do.

It was quiet as they exited–-only one Death Eater was outside of the door and Hermione stunned him, running past quickly with Bellatrix at her heels. They fell into the elevator, and Hermione drew in deep, sharp breaths as she fumbled with the keys, pressing  the floor of the Auror’s office before she stood back, looking at the witch. For the first time, she really took in the woman’s appearance.

The scars on her wrist had disappeared and even her hand nails had regained a youthful healthy look. Her face was fuller, softer and even her eyes seemed less cruel than they had, moment ago when Hermione had been certain she was facing death. Could this really be the same woman?
I think it erased your memories,' she answered.

Bellatrix paused, her brow lowering as she looked away––for a moment, Hermione thought she saw the woman’s eyes glass over, as if she was about to cry. 'Only a feeling of terror,' Bellatrix admitted, softly.

The elevator hummed, shifting as it began move along its path. 'It’s...complicated, but I’ll try to explain everything once my friends are safe.' Bellatrix stared back at her, a strange expression on her face before she nodded. Hermione looked away, up to where she could the counter for what floors they were passing. They were nearly there, only a few more floors to go. It was crazy, absolutely mad that she was in an elevator with the Bellatrix Lestrange. But the woman was quiet, standing with her back tall, her long wild hair flowing down her back.

She even seemed a little taller––which was strange. Maybe her back had curved in Azkaban. Despite Hermione’s feelings towards people like Bellatrix, the way Azkaban treated its prisoners set Hermione’s teeth on edge. It didn’t matter what the crime was, everyone went there and faced the same cold, torturous experience.

Was it any wonder that the woman that came out of that seemed more mad than when she went in it? And then, Hermione realised that she was taking Bellatrix Lestrange to the very people who would want to place her back in that hell hole.
'I can’t explain why but I’m going to change your appearance, okay? I promise it’s for your safety.' Bellatrix turned and look at her, frowning.
Given her youthful appearance, Hermione hoped it would be enough.

To the Auror’s on night shift, they seemed more alarmed that there was Hogwarts students in their office. When Hermione explained quickly, misleading them that they had been trying to stop others who were wannabe death eaters had tried to sneak into to the Department of Mysteries as an initiation with older Dark Wizards, the Aurors were skeptical but not entirely disbelieving her story.
'Miss Granger, if there’s nothing down there, you’ll be in a lot of trouble.'
The man’s eyes narrowed but he waived to another colleague who nodded, walking off to where Hermione hoped would be some way to convey a message to Dumbledore.
It was a simple clock, with its numbers written in black.
There was nothing about it that should fascinate the other girl and yet it held her attention all the same. But, perhaps there was more to the question.
'He’s a good man,' Hermione said.

A quiet presided them and Hermione felt the exhaustion of what had occurred weigh through her. She was tired and yet the idea of sleeping made her stomach twist. 'Someone’s here,' Bellatrix whispered to her. Hermione, turning, looking to where the office doors had just closed.
Hermione jumped off the seat, running forward she threw her arms around Remus and hugged him close.
Hermione took a breath and then felt herself begin to cry. Dreams of the Department of Mysteries plagued Hermione all Summer.

In reality the pattern is closer to a Gaussian, or normal distribution, with a higher density in the center that tapers off at the edges. Patterns are usually measured by firing at a 30-inch (76 cm) diameter circle on a large sheet of paper placed at varying distances. The hits inside the circle are counted, and compared to the total number of pellets, and the density of the pattern inside the circle is examined. An "ideal" pattern would put nearly 100% of the pellets in the circle and would have no voids—any region where a target silhouette will fit and not cover 3 or more holes is considered a potential problem.
The choke typically consists of a conical section that smoothly tapers from the bore diameter down to the choke diameter, followed by a cylindrical section of the choke diameter.

These are free websites that post links to commercial sites, providing a sampling of the commercial site in the form of thumbnail images, or in the form of Free Hosted Galleries—samplings of full-sized content provided and hosted by the commercial sites to promote their site.
When a user purchases a subscription to a commercial site after clicking through from a free thumbnail gallery site, the commercial site makes a payment to the owner of the free site.
These sites are called a thumbnail gallery post (TGP). As a rule, these sites sort thumbnails by category and type of content available on a linked gallery.

However, TGP sites are open to abuse, with the most abusive form being the so-called CJ (abbreviation for circlejerk), that contains links that mislead the surfer to sites he or she actually did not wish to see. This is also called a redirect.

Linklists, unlike TGP/MGP sites, do not display a huge number of pictures. A linklist is a (frequently) categorised web list of links to so-called "freesites*", but unlike TGPs, links are provided in a form of text, not thumbs. It is still a question which form is more descriptive to a surfer, but many webmasters cite a trend that thumbs are much more productive, and simplify searching. On the other hand, linklists have a larger amount of unique text, which helps them improve their positions in search engine listings. TopLists are linklists whose internal ranking of freesites is based on incoming traffic from those freesites, except that freesites designed for TopLists have many more galleries.

One of the most famous medieval references comes from the English poet Geoffrey Chaucer.

Many were friendly and inclusive. In families, people sometimes gave greetings to parents, siblings, or other relatives. In recent times, this broader understanding has become even more visible.

The heart is perhaps the most recognizable. The stylized heart shape is not a scientifically accurate drawing of the human organ. Instead, it developed over centuries as a visual emblem of emotion, devotion, and desire.
It functions almost instantly as a sign of affection.
According to myth, those struck by his arrows fall in love.
This connection developed through classical associations, literary symbolism, and later floral traditions.
The giving of flowers combines beauty, symbolism, and temporary display. It is one of the clearest examples of how emotion is expressed through objects.
Confectionery companies helped popularize the pairing of sweets and romance.
Chocolate carries its own long cultural history, tied to luxury, celebration, and sensory pleasure.
In some cultures, these small edible messages are especially associated with the holiday. Their phrases are often playful, brief, and formulaic.
Red is often linked to passion, desire, and strong emotion.

These colors are now so strongly tied to the holiday that they shape retail displays, fashion choices, packaging, and event design each year. Their repetition helps create a recognizable seasonal atmosphere.
This strong branding is one reason the holiday remains commercially powerful.
Restaurants often offer special menus.

In the United Kingdom, many similar customs exist, though practices and emphasis can vary by generation and region. In some Latin American countries, comparable celebrations may focus on love and friendship together.

South Korea developed its own related customs, including additional themed days. These examples show that the holiday is not a single uniform tradition but a flexible framework that adapts to local culture.
In others, it has met criticism or resistance.
Some political or cultural groups have criticized it as foreign influence or excessive consumerism.
Yet even where official disapproval exists, people may still adopt parts of the holiday privately. This tension between public norms and personal feeling is part of the holiday’s global story.

Businesses did not invent the holiday, but they expanded and standardized it. Advertising encouraged people to see gift-giving as a proper expression of care. Over time, certain expectations became normalized.

Critics argue that this process can reduce emotion to spending. They say it pressures people to perform affection according to market rules. Supporters respond that gifts can still be meaningful and that rituals help people express what they genuinely feel. Both views capture part of the truth.
Its social power comes partly from that combination.
Many people enjoy it and look forward to it each year. Others feel left out, pressured, or annoyed by its messaging.
People in difficult relationships may find it painful rather than joyful.
Because of this, alternative observances have emerged.
It involved rituals related to fertility, purification, and the coming of spring.
Modern historians tend to treat this claim with caution. There may be broad seasonal overlap, and later Christian authorities did reshape parts of the calendar in relation to earlier customs.
This is a good example of how holiday histories are often simplified in popular retellings.

These stories are emotionally powerful, which helps explain their persistence. However, historians do not treat them as firmly established facts. They belong more to the realm of hagiography and devotional legend. Still, legends can shape culture even when their historical basis is uncertain.
Local observance remained possible. This change shows the difference between church liturgy and popular custom.
Its popularity did not depend on formal church observance.
Even modern songs, films, and television specials contribute to the holiday’s atmosphere.
This is one reason the holiday remains adaptable. It absorbs new styles of expression while preserving old motifs.
In the digital age, text messages, emails, social media posts, digital cards, video calls, and online gift orders have become common.

At the same time, some people value handwritten notes more than ever because they feel rare and personal. This contrast between convenience and sincerity is characteristic of modern celebration. Technology makes expression easier, but it also raises questions about authenticity and effort.
Businesses segment their audiences carefully, targeting couples, singles, friends, parents, and pet owners.
The holiday’s commercial range has widened significantly.
This expansion reflects both market strategy and cultural change. It allows more people to participate, though it also increases the sense that no part of life is beyond commercialization.
In many places, children exchange small cards, candies, or crafts with classmates.

At the same time, schools sometimes need to manage concerns about exclusion or uneven gift-giving. Rules may require students to bring cards for everyone or for no one in particular. This shows how even a seemingly simple holiday practice involves social structure and emotional management.

Cards, posters, advertisements, films, cartoons, packaging, and decorations all contribute to its iconography. Lace borders, roses, envelopes, cupids, arrows, hearts, doves, swans, and candlelit scenes are common motifs. These images reinforce the idea that love can be represented through a familiar symbolic vocabulary.

Contemporary designs may range from elegant and minimalist to ironic and humorous. Despite these shifts, the core symbols remain recognizable. This continuity gives the holiday a strong visual identity across eras.

Many cards rely on jokes, puns, exaggerated sweetness, or deliberate silliness. This comic side helps balance the emotional pressure of the holiday. It allows people to participate without making a grand confession. It also makes the holiday useful in many social settings where full seriousness would feel awkward.
Humor helps the holiday function across a wide range of relationships.
For them, meaningful relationships are shown in daily life rather than special occasions. This debate is part of the holiday’s broader social meaning.
Other believers choose not to celebrate the day at all.

Others avoid it due to moral concerns or because it is seen as imported custom. These differences show that holidays do not carry one single meaning. They are interpreted through local values, family habits, and personal beliefs.
Many of these expressions are conventional.
Ritual phrases help people say things that are otherwise difficult to express. Even cliché has social function. It provides a script for feeling.
It is also about public expression.

It tells people when to express affection and how that expression might look. That structure can be comforting, but it can also be constraining. The same ritual that enables feeling can standardize it.
In many cultures, customs have historically assumed different roles for men and women in giving gifts or initiating romantic gestures.
Some of these patterns have weakened over time, but not all of them have disappeared.
Public sharing can be joyful, but it can also intensify comparison. A private holiday becomes partly public again through online display. This can amplify both pleasure and insecurity.

Others prefer to keep it personal. Social media has made the holiday more visible than ever. It has also turned small rituals into content. This shift is part of the larger transformation of everyday life by digital platforms.

It arrives in the late winter of the Northern Hemisphere, a period often associated with cold weather, bare landscapes, and anticipation of spring. This timing helps explain why imagery of warmth, flowers, and renewal feels so powerful.

In older agricultural societies, seasonal change mattered deeply. Even if modern urban celebrations are less tied to nature, traces of that seasonal symbolism remain. Love and spring continue to be linked in imagination. The pairing feels ancient even when expressed through modern products.

Some argue that it promotes shallow sentiment. Others say it turns relationships into competitions. Still others object to its exclusionary focus on couples. Satire of the holiday is common in newspapers, television, and online culture.

This contradiction is revealing. People may dislike the pressure or clichés while still valuing the chance to mark affection. Criticism has not destroyed the holiday. In some ways, it has become part of the holiday itself.

Some communities emphasize handmade gifts. Some families cook particular foods. Some couples revisit the place where they met. Some friends organize gatherings specifically to avoid spending the day alone.
These ordinary practices matter because they show how holidays are lived, not just marketed.
Everyday history includes personal habits, family traditions, and small acts of care.
Unlike birthdays or anniversaries, it is not tied to one individual life story.

Nearly everyone encounters it in some way, even indirectly. It is difficult to avoid entirely in societies where it is widely observed. This public quality explains why reactions to the holiday are so strong. It speaks to universal experiences, but not everyone experiences them the same way.
It can sound sweet, old-fashioned, playful, or dramatic depending on context.
That shift is itself historically remarkable. Very few saints’ names have become so fully absorbed into secular emotional language.
No single origin story explains it fully. Attempts to reduce it to one source usually miss the complexity of cultural history.
New technologies will alter how messages are sent.
Environmental concerns may influence attitudes toward cut flowers, packaging, and consumption. Cultural diversity will keep generating new interpretations.
Human beings continue to seek occasions for expressing care.
Others will ignore it entirely.
It is important because it reveals how societies organize feeling.
It connects private emotion to public ritual.
It can feel tender, commercial, joyful, awkward, intimate, theatrical, or exhausting. Often it feels like several of these things at once. That complexity is exactly why it has endured.
It is a mirror of how different times and places have imagined human affection itself.

The global economy suffered heavily from the war, although participating nations were affected differently. The agreement created the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development (IBRD), which later became part of the World Bank Group.

Recovery began with the mid-1948 currency reform in West Germany, and was sped up by the liberalisation of European economic policy that the US Marshall Plan economic aid (1948–1951) both directly and indirectly caused.

By contrast, the United Kingdom was in a state of economic ruin, and although receiving a quarter of the total Marshall Plan assistance, more than any other European country, it continued in relative economic decline for decades.
China returned to its pre-war industrial production by 1952.
Civilian deaths have been estimated to comprise 67% to 80% of all direct and indirect deaths from the war.
Of the 20 million to 25 million military deaths in the war, the majority were of German and Soviet soldiers, including prisoners of war (POWs), on the Eastern Front.
The high civilian death toll relative to military deaths was unusual for major wars up to that time.

Although Japanese forces were sometimes welcomed as liberators from European domination, Japanese war crimes frequently turned local public opinion against them. During Japan's initial conquest, it captured 4,000,000 barrels (640,000 m3) of oil (~550,000 tonnes) left behind by retreating Allied forces; and by 1943, was able to get production in the Dutch East Indies up to 50 million barrels (7,900,000 m3) of oil (~6.8 million tonnes), 76 percent of its 1940 output rate.

In Asia at the same time, China had roughly six times the population of Japan but only an 89 percent higher GDP; this reduces to three times the population and only a 38 percent higher GDP if Japanese colonies are included.
Aircraft were used for reconnaissance, as fighters, bombers, and ground-support, and each role developed considerably.

Anti-aircraft weaponry also advanced, including defences such as radar and surface-to-air artillery, in particular the introduction of the proximity fuze. The use of the jet aircraft was pioneered and led to jets becoming standard in air forces worldwide.

The tank, which had been used predominantly for infantry support in the First World War, had evolved into the primary weapon. Economic reconstruction became a central challenge after 1945. Much of Europe and parts of Asia lay in ruins. Housing, transport, agriculture, and industry had been devastated. Medical advances, including antibiotics and new approaches to trauma care, saved lives during and after the war.

In many countries, the role of the state expanded dramatically through economic planning, conscription, rationing, propaganda, and mass mobilization. Women took on work and public responsibilities that challenged older gender norms, though postwar societies often tried to restore prewar patterns.

Survivors faced enormous challenges after liberation, including illness, trauma, homelessness, and the loss of entire families and communities. Many Jewish survivors spent months or years in displaced persons camps before emigrating or rebuilding lives elsewhere.

Euphemisms such as special treatment, evacuation, resettlement, and final solution disguised murder behind administrative terms. Bureaucratic speech reduced people to categories and problems.

Policy was not made only in Berlin and then mechanically implemented elsewhere. The eastern occupied lands in particular became laboratories of racial domination, starvation policy, anti-partisan warfare, and genocidal practice.

Entire towns and neighborhoods lost their populations. The demographic, cultural, and psychological consequences lasted for generations. Survivors carried trauma that affected families and communities long after the war. Countries across Europe were left to reckon not only with occupation and loss but with collaboration, indifference, rescue, guilt, and silence.

In January 2024, the International Energy Agency (IEA) released Electricity 2024, Analysis and Forecast to 2026, forecasting electric power use. This is the first IEA report to make projections for data centers and power consumption for artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency. The report states that power demand for these uses might double by 2026, with additional electric power usage equal to electricity used by the whole Japanese nation.

There is a feverish rise in the construction of data centers throughout the US, making large technology firms (e.g., Microsoft, Meta, Google, Amazon) into voracious consumers of electric power. Projected electric consumption is so immense that there is concern that it will be fulfilled no matter the source. A ChatGPT search involves the use of 10 times the electrical energy as a Google search. The large firms are in haste to find power sources – from nuclear energy to geothermal to fusion.

A 2024 Goldman Sachs Research Paper, AI Data Centers and the Coming US Power Demand Surge, found "US power demand (is) likely to experience growth not seen in a generation...." and forecasts that, by 2030, US data centers will consume 8% of US power, as opposed to 3% in 2022, presaging growth for the electrical power generation industry by a variety of means. Data centers' need for more and more electrical power is such that they might max out the electrical grid.

In 2024, the Wall Street Journal reported that big AI companies have begun negotiations with the US nuclear power providers to provide electricity to the data centers. In March 2024 Amazon purchased a Pennsylvania nuclear-powered data center for US$650 million. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said nuclear power is a good option for the data centers.

In September 2024, Microsoft announced an agreement with Constellation Energy to re-open the Three Mile Island nuclear power plant to provide Microsoft with 100% of all electric power produced by the plant for 20 years. Reopening the plant, which suffered a partial nuclear meltdown of its Unit 2 reactor in 1979, will require Constellation to get through strict regulatory processes which will include extensive safety scrutiny from the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission. If approved (this will be the first ever US re-commissioning of a nuclear plant), over 835 megawatts of power – enough for 800,000 homes – of energy will be produced. The cost for re-opening and upgrading is estimated at US$1.6 billion and is dependent on tax breaks for nuclear power contained in the 2022 US Inflation Reduction Act. The US government and the state of Michigan are investing almost US$2 billion to reopen the Palisades Nuclear reactor on Lake Michigan. Closed since 2022, the plant is planned to be reopened in October 2025. The Three Mile Island facility will be renamed the Crane Clean Energy Center after Chris Crane, a nuclear proponent and former CEO of Exelon who was responsible for Exelon's spinoff of Constellation.

After the last approval in September 2023, Taiwan suspended the approval of data centers north of Taoyuan with a capacity of more than 5 MW in 2024, due to power supply shortages. Taiwan aims to phase out nuclear power by 2025. On the other hand, Singapore imposed a ban on the opening of data centers in 2019 due to electric power, but in 2022, lifted this ban.

On 1 November 2024, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) rejected an application submitted by Talen Energy for approval to supply some electricity from the nuclear power station Susquehanna to Amazon's data center. According to the Commission Chairman Willie L. Phillips, it is a burden on the electricity grid as well as a significant cost shifting concern to households and other business sectors.

In 2025, a report prepared by the International Energy Agency estimated the greenhouse gas emissions from the energy consumption of AI at 180 million tons. On 28 June 2015, Google Photos's new image labeling feature mistakenly identified Jacky Alcine and a friend as "gorillas" because they were black. The system was trained on a dataset that contained very few images of black people, a problem called "sample size disparity". Google "fixed" this problem by preventing the system from labelling anything as a "gorilla". Eight years later, in 2023, Google Photos still could not identify a gorilla, and neither could similar products from Apple, Facebook, Microsoft and Amazon.

COMPAS is a commercial program widely used by U.S. courts to assess the likelihood of a defendant becoming a recidivist. In 2016, Julia Angwin at ProPublica discovered that COMPAS exhibited racial bias, despite the fact that the program was not told the races of the defendants. Although the error rate for both whites and blacks was calibrated equal at exactly 61%, the errors for each race were different—the system consistently overestimated the chance that a black person would re-offend and would underestimate the chance that a white person would not re-offend. In 2017, several researchers[l] showed that it was mathematically impossible for COMPAS to accommodate all possible measures of fairness when the base rates of re-offense were different for whites and blacks in the data.

A program can make biased decisions even if the data does not explicitly mention a problematic feature (such as "race" or "gender"). The feature will correlate with other features (like "address", "shopping history" or "first name"), and the program will make the same decisions based on these features as it would on "race" or "gender". Moritz Hardt said "the most robust fact in this research area is that fairness through blindness doesn't work."
There are various conflicting definitions and mathematical models of fairness.
The subjectivity in the notions of bias and fairness makes it difficult for companies to operationalize them.

Another machine learning system designed to help effectively allocate medical resources was found to classify patients with asthma as being at "low risk" of dying from pneumonia. Having asthma is actually a severe risk factor, but since the patients having asthma would usually get much more medical care, they were relatively unlikely to die according to the training data. The correlation between asthma and low risk of dying from pneumonia was real, but misleading.

Common mitigation measures during the public health emergency included travel restrictions, lockdowns, business restrictions and closures, workplace hazard controls, mask mandates, quarantines, testing systems, and contact tracing of the infected.

The pandemic caused severe social and economic disruption around the world, including the largest global recession since the Great Depression. Widespread supply shortages, including food shortages, were caused by supply chain disruptions and panic buying. Reduced human activity led to an unprecedented temporary decrease in pollution. Educational institutions and public areas were partially or fully closed in many jurisdictions, and many events were cancelled or postponed during 2020 and 2021. Telework became much more common for white-collar workers as the pandemic evolved. Misinformation circulated through social media and occasionally through mass media, and political tensions intensified. The pandemic raised issues of racial and geographic discrimination, health equity, and the balance between public health imperatives and individual rights.
Different definitions of pandemics lead to different determinations of when they end.
In epidemiology, a pandemic is defined as "an epidemic occurring over a very wide area, crossing international boundaries, and usually affecting a large number of people".

The end of a pandemic or other epidemic only rarely involves the total disappearance of a disease, and historically, much less attention has been given to defining the ends of epidemics than their beginnings. The ends of particular epidemics have been defined in a variety of ways, differing according to academic field, and differently based on location and social group. An epidemic's end can be considered a social phenomenon, not just a biological one.

The initial practice of naming them according to where the variants were identified (e.g. Delta began as the "Indian variant") is no longer common. A more systematic naming scheme reflects the variant's PANGO lineage (e.g., Omicron's lineage is B.1.1.529) and is used for other variants.

Measures ranged from public advisories to stringent lockdowns. Outbreak control strategies are divided into elimination and mitigation.
These initial strategies can be pursued sequentially or simultaneously during the acquired immunity phase through natural and vaccine-induced immunity.
Containment is undertaken to stop an outbreak from spreading into the general population.
The people they have interacted with are contacted and isolated for long enough to ensure that they are either not infected or no longer contagious. Screening is the starting point for containment.
Successful containment or suppression reduces Rt to less than 1.
Should containment fail, efforts focus on mitigation: measures taken to slow the spread and limit its effects on the healthcare system and society.

This decreases the risk of overwhelming health services and provides more time for developing vaccines and treatments. Individual behaviour changed in many jurisdictions. Many people worked from home instead of at their traditional workplaces.

Non-pharmaceutical interventions that may reduce spread include personal actions such as wearing face masks, self-quarantine, and hand hygiene; community measures aimed at reducing interpersonal contacts such as closing workplaces and schools and cancelling large gatherings; community engagement to encourage acceptance and participation in such interventions; as well as environmental measures such as surface cleaning.

More drastic actions, such as quarantining entire populations and strict travel bans have been attempted in various jurisdictions. The Chinese and Australian government approaches have included many lockdowns and are widely considered the most strict. The New Zealand government response included the most severe travel restrictions. As part of its K-Quarantine programme, South Korea introduced mass screening and localised quarantines, and issued alerts on the movements of infected individuals. The Singaporean government's response included so-called "circuit breaker lockdowns" and financial support for those affected while also imposing large fines for those who broke quarantine.

Contact tracing attempts to identify recent contacts of newly infected individuals, and to screen them for infection; the traditional approach is to request a list of contacts from infectees, and then telephone or visit the contacts. Contact tracing was widely used during the Western African Ebola virus epidemic in 2014.

Another approach is to collect location data from mobile devices to identify those who have come in significant contact with infectees, which prompted privacy concerns. On 10 April 2020, Google and Apple announced an initiative for privacy-preserving contact tracing.

WHO described increasing capacity and adapting healthcare as a fundamental mitigation. The ECDC and WHO's European regional office issued guidelines for hospitals and primary healthcare services for shifting resources at multiple levels, including focusing laboratory services towards testing, cancelling elective procedures, separating and isolating patients, and increasing intensive care capabilities by training personnel and increasing ventilators and beds. The pandemic drove widespread adoption of telehealth.

Due to supply chain capacity limitations, some manufacturers began 3D printing material such as nasal swabs and ventilator parts. In one example, an Italian startup received legal threats due to alleged patent infringement after reverse-engineering and printing one hundred requested ventilator valves overnight. Individuals and groups of makers created and shared open source designs, and manufacturing devices using locally sourced materials, sewing, and 3D printing. Millions of face shields, protective gowns, and masks were made. Other ad hoc medical supplies included shoe covers, surgical caps, powered air-purifying respirators, and hand sanitiser. Novel devices were created such as ear savers, non-invasive ventilation helmets, and ventilator splitters.
In July 2021, several experts expressed concern that achieving herd immunity may not be possible because Delta can transmit among vaccinated individuals.
Consequently, WHO and CDC encouraged vaccinated people to continue with non-pharmaceutical interventions such as masking, social distancing, and quarantining if exposed.

The WHO does not make official declarations of when pandemics end. The decision came after Tedros convened with the International Health Regulations Emergency Committee, wherein the Committee noted that due to the decrease in deaths and hospitalisations, and the prevalence of vaccinations and the level of general immunity, it was time to remove the emergency designation and "transition to long-term management". Tedros agreed, and the WHO reduced the classification to an "established and ongoing health issue". In a press conference, Tedros remarked that the diminishing threat from COVID-19 had "allowed most countries to return to life as we knew it before COVID-19".
